children.title: The Science of Refractive Surgery
Corneal Optics
Cornea provides ~43D of total ~60D eye power; most refraction occurs at air-tear film interface (largest refractive index change)

Normal cornea: prolate shape (steeper centrally, flatter peripherally); Q value ~-0.26; negative Q = prolate; post-myopic LASIK cornea becomes oblate (flatter centrally, steeper peripherally) → increased spherical aberration

Anterior surface: main refracting surface (~48D); posterior surface: contributes ~-6D (subtracts from total); instruments that measure only anterior cornea (keratometry, Placido topography) tend to overestimate total corneal power by ignoring posterior
Total corneal power (TCP): requires both anterior and posterior measurements; Scheimpflug (Pentacam) measures both; critical for IOL calculation post-refractive surgery
Wavefront Analysis & Aberrations
Lower-Order Aberrations (LOA)
Comprise ~90% of total ocular aberrations; fully correctable with spectacles/CLs
Z₂⁰ = defocus (sphere); Z₂±² = astigmatism (regular cylinder)
Residual defocus after refractive surgery = regression/undercorrection
Higher-Order Aberrations (HOA)
Not correctable with spherocylindrical correction; measured by wavefront aberrometer
Z₃±¹ = coma (asymmetric blur; comet-shaped PSF; causes decentration symptoms)
Z₃±³ = trefoil
Z₄⁰ = spherical aberration (SA); peripheral rays focus differently from paraxial; causes halos, glare; INCREASES after myopic LASIK (oblate cornea)
Z₄±² = secondary astigmatism; Z₄±⁴ = quadrafoil
Root mean square (RMS): total HOA magnitude; normal eye HOA RMS ~0.3μm; >0.5μm symptomatic
Zernike polynomials: mathematical orthogonal functions describing wavefront shape over pupil; each term = specific aberration
Wavefront Measurement
Hartmann-Shack sensor: grid of lenslets focuses wavefront onto CCD; deviation of spots from ideal grid = local wavefront slope; reconstructs wavefront map
Tscherning aberrometer: grid of dots projected onto retina; deviation of dot pattern = wavefront error
Ray tracing: individual laser beams sequentially projected; refraction measured at each location
Limitations: poor quality with irregular corneas (KC, post-LASIK), media opacities, small pupils <4mm (insufficient data points)
Corneal Imaging
Corneal Topography (Placido-based)
Measures anterior corneal surface curvature; concentric ring reflection from anterior tear film; Placido disc

Map types: axial/sagittal (most used; shows overall curvature; smoothed), tangential/instantaneous (most sensitive for localised irregularities; best for KC screening), elevation (height above reference sphere; best for posterior ectasia)
Colour scale: warm colours (red/orange) = steeper; cool (blue) = flatter; ABSOLUTE (fixed scale, compare between patients) vs NORMALISED (relative to exam, more detail)
Normal: regular bowtie (WTR astigmatism); symmetric bowtie; inferior steepening → keratoconus suspect
KISA% index (Rabinowitz): KC screening; uses K2-K1, inferior-superior difference, inferior skewed radial axis; >60% = KC; 60-100% = suspect
Corneal Tomography (Scheimpflug + Placido)
Scheimpflug camera (Pentacam HR, Galilei): rotating camera captures 25-50 cross-sections; anterior + posterior surfaces + pachymetry + ACD; true elevation maps
Posterior elevation map: most sensitive for early ectasia; posterior surface changes before anterior in forme fruste KC
BAD-D (Belin-Ambrosio Display): composite score incorporating anterior elevation, posterior elevation, pachymetry progression, best-fit sphere deviation; D score >1.6 = high ectasia risk
Pachymetric map: thinnest point location; normal 520-540μm central; displaced thinnest point (infero-temporal) → KC suspect; minimum pachymetry value for LASIK ≥480-500μm
Indications for tomography in refractive surgery: ALL LASIK/PRK candidates; detect forme fruste KC, pellucid marginal degeneration, post-LASIK ectasia risk
Corneal Biomechanics
Stroma provides structural strength: interwoven collagen lamellae; anterior third has more interlacing → greater stiffness; posterior two-thirds more parallel lamellae → less stiffness
Residual stromal bed (RSB): stromal tissue remaining after flap and ablation; minimum 250-300μm to prevent ectasia; RSB < 250μm = high ectasia risk
Measurement

Ocular Response Analyser (ORA; Reichert): air-pulse tonometry; measures 2 pressure readings as cornea deforms and recovers; CH (corneal hysteresis) = energy absorption = viscoelastic dampening; CRF (corneal resistance factor) = overall stiffness; CH reduced in KC, post-LASIK, glaucoma

Corvis ST (Oculus): air-puff + Scheimpflug high-speed imaging; dynamic corneal response parameters; DA ratio (deformation amplitude ratio) = best single biomechanical KC discriminator; TBI (Tomographic-Biomechanical Index): combines tomography + biomechanics

Corneal crosslinking (CXL): riboflavin + UV-A; creates new collagen cross-links; increases CH and CRF; halts KC progression; standard Dresden protocol: epithelium removed, 0.1% riboflavin + dextran, 370nm UV-A 3mW/cm² ×30 min (total 5.4 J/cm²)
Laser Biophysics

Excimer laser (ArF): 193nm UV light; photodecomposition (photoablation); breaks molecular bonds directly (C-C bonds of stromal collagen); cold process; no heat generation → precise ablation without thermal damage; ablates ~0.25μm per pulse

Femtosecond laser: 1053nm infrared; ultrashort pulses (10⁻¹⁵ s); photodisruption (ionisation → plasma → shock wave → tissue separation); used for LASIK flap creation, SMILE lenticule, arcuate keratotomy; precise intrastromal cuts with minimal thermal damage

Munnerlyn formula: ablation depth (μm) = D × S² / 3; D = dioptre correction, S = optical zone diameter (mm); each additional dioptre of myopia or each mm increase in OZ = significant increase in tissue removal
Fluence (J/cm²): energy per unit area; determines ablation efficiency; controlled by pulse energy + spot size; calibration essential before each procedure
Corneal Wound Healing

Surface ablation wound healing: epithelial removal → keratocyte apoptosis in anterior stroma → myofibroblast activation (TGF-β) → haze formation (scar tissue) → re-epithelialisation (3-5 days) → keratocyte repopulation
Corneal haze: subepithelial fibrosis; more in PRK (surface ablation) than LASIK; higher with greater ablation depth; prevented by MMC 0.02% intraoperatively (applied for 12-60 seconds post-ablation)
LASIK wound healing: flap interface heals minimally; stromal bed heals by keratocyte repopulation without myofibroblast activation (no TGF-β) → minimal haze
Regression: gradual return toward original refractive error; more in high corrections; from epithelial hyperplasia (surface ablation) + keratocyte/collagen remodelling

MMC: anti-metabolite; inhibits keratocyte proliferation; prevents myofibroblast transformation; essential for high-myopia PRK, retreatment PRK, post-LASIK haze treatment; avoid in thin corneas (toxic to endothelium if concentration too high or duration too long)
Patient Evaluation
Patient Selection Criteria
Age: typically ≥21 years (stable refraction); ≥18 for selected stable cases; upper limit: consider RLE after ~45-50 (presbyopia management)
Stable refraction: <0.5D change per year for ≥1-2 years; exclude progressive myopia, uncontrolled diabetes, pregnancy (corneal changes)
Realistic expectations: understand: halos/glare (especially multifocal ablations), regression, dry eye (especially LASIK), need for reading glasses post-surgery (presbyopia), residual error possible
Absolute Contraindications
Keratoconus (frank or forme fruste): progressive ectasia risk
Thin cornea: residual stromal bed <250μm post-ablation; total pachymetry <480μm (LASIK) or <400μm (PRK)
Active ocular disease: herpetic keratitis (active), severe dry eye, pellucid marginal degeneration (controversial), autoimmune corneal disease
Unstable refraction: change >0.5D/year
Unrealistic expectations or inability to consent
Relative Contraindications
Autoimmune/collagen vascular disease: RA, SLE, Sjögren (impaired wound healing; dry eye risk); PRK > LASIK if proceeding
Controlled diabetes: poor wound healing; corneal neuropathy; increased infection risk; PRK > LASIK
HIV: caution if immunocompromised; otherwise treat as normal if stable
Pregnancy/nursing: refractive changes; delay until 3 months post-partum and 3 cycles of stable refraction
Amblyopia: will not improve with refractive surgery; counsel accordingly; benefit still for anisometropic correction
Large pupils (>7mm in mesopic): higher risk of halos/glare with standard ablation zones
High correction: >-10D myopia (tissue removal limits), >+4D hyperopia (less predictable)
Preoperative Examination
Uncorrected VA + BCVA: establishes baseline; BCVA must be achievable (reduced BCVA = other pathology)
Manifest + cycloplegic refraction: compare; large difference (>0.75D sphere) = significant latent hyperopia; always treat cycloplegic in hyperopic corrections; use manifest in myopia
Dominant eye determination: important for monovision planning; +1D fogging test
Corneal topography + tomography: mandatory; screen for KC, PMD, irregular astigmatism; posterior elevation map critical
Pachymetry: central + minimum; must calculate RSB; optical pachymetry (Pentacam, AS-OCT) preferred; ultrasound alternative
Wavefront aberrometry: guides treatment type (wavefront-guided vs optimised); poor quality scan → contraindication for wavefront-guided
Pupil size: mesopic pupil diameter; predict dysphotopsia risk; align ablation zone accordingly
IOP: measure before surgery and account for underestimation after (post-LASIK IOP readings falsely low by 4-6mmHg)
Dry eye evaluation: TBUT, Schirmer, meibomian gland assessment; treat moderate-severe dry eye before proceeding; LASIK worsens dry eye more than PRK; SMILE least dry eye
Dilated fundus exam: lattice degeneration (RD risk post-refractive surgery with myopia); peripheral breaks; macular disease; optic disc
Contact Lens Discontinuation
Soft CLs: discontinue 2-7 days (ideally 1-2 weeks) before evaluation and surgery; allow cornea to return to natural shape
RGP/hard CLs: discontinue 2-4 weeks minimum; longer if worn many years (corneal moulding)
Orthokeratology: discontinue 4+ weeks; longer for higher corrections; confirm stable topography before surgery
Incisional Corneal Surgery
Radial Keratotomy (RK)
Historical; radial incisions 4-16 cuts; 90-95% depth; peripheral to central clear zone (3-4mm); flatten central cornea → reduce myopia; largely replaced by excimer laser

Postoperative effects: hyperopic drift (progressive flattening over years); diurnal fluctuation (morning myopia, evening hyperopia from corneal hydration changes); unstable refraction; weakened corneal structure
Complications: undercorrection (most common), overcorrection, progressive hyperopia, glare/starburst (weakened incision scars), corneal perforation, infection, irregular astigmatism

Patients with prior RK: IOL calculation difficult (flattened anterior surface, altered K/posterior ratio); ASCRS online calculator essential; unpredictable outcomes; warn of hyperopic surprise; more than post-LASIK challenge
Arcuate Keratotomy (AK) & Limbal Relaxing Incisions (LRI)
Curved incisions at optical zone (AK) or limbus (LRI) to flatten steep meridian; correct astigmatism by coupling effect
Coupling: flattening effect in incision meridian paired with steepening in perpendicular meridian; coupling ratio ~1:1 at limbus; varies with OZ
DACE procedure (direction, axis, centration, effect): guides planning; nomogram-based (Donnenfeld, Nichamin, NAPA)
AK parameters: arc length (more arc = more effect), optical zone (smaller OZ = more effect), depth (90-95% corneal thickness), number of incisions
LRI: at limbus (6-7mm OZ); less effect per mm arc than corneal; safer (further from visual axis); preferred for <1.5D astigmatism at time of cataract surgery
Femtosecond AK: more precise depth control; lower perforation risk; FLACS allows intraoperative AK
Effect: up to ~1.5-3D correction of astigmatism; less accurate than toric IOL for higher astigmatism
Photoablation: Techniques & Outcomes
Excimer Laser Fundamentals
ArF excimer: 193nm; 10-15ns pulses; spot sizes 0.25-6.5mm (scanning slit or flying spot); broad-beam systems (older) vs scanning spot (most modern)
Flying spot (small gaussian beam): 0.5-1mm spot; high repetition rate; smooth ablation; less thermal effect; current standard
Laser calibration: PMMA test plates; calibrate ablation depth and profile before each session; eye tracker calibration; temperature/humidity affect ablation rate (nomogram adjustments)
Eye tracking: active tracking follows pupil/limbus centre during treatment; IR pupil camera; 500-1000Hz; compensates for involuntary eye movements; critical for ablation centration
Surface Ablation (PRK, LASEK, epi-LASIK, TransPRK)
PRK (Photorefractive Keratectomy)
Epithelium removed manually (blade/brush/alcohol/spatula) → Bowman exposed → ablation → bandage CL placed
Advantages: no flap (no flap complications, stronger biomechanics, better for thin corneas, dry eye less severe long-term, suitable for contact sports/military)
Disadvantages: painful (2-5 days), slower visual recovery (1-4 weeks), haze risk (higher corrections), MMC required for >-5D, cannot enhance easily (open Bowman)
LASEK: alcohol 20% loosens epithelial sheet as flap; fold aside → ablation → replace epithelial flap; faster healing than PRK; less pain; epithelial flap usually removed anyway
TransPRK (no-touch transepithelial PRK): single laser step ablates epithelium + stroma without physical removal; Schwind AMARIS; faster; least handling; increasing popularity

MMC application: 0.02-0.04% mitomycin C; sponge applied to stroma for 12-60 seconds after ablation (before CL placement); prevents myofibroblast activation; essential for >-5D, retreatment, previous haze; rinse copiously afterward
LASIK
Flap Creation
Microkeratome (mechanical): oscillating blade; metal or single-use; 100-180μm flap thickness; ring + suction; risk: incomplete flap, buttonhole flap (steep cornea), free cap, irregular cut

Femtosecond laser flap (IntraLASIK): precise programmable thickness (90-140μm); planar geometry; side cut angle customisable; vertically cut edges (better wound sealing); lower incidence of flap complications; still risk of OBL (opaque bubble layer)
Flap dimensions: diameter 8.5-9.5mm; hinge nasally or superiorly; 300-360μm minimum flap thickness above hinge
Steps

1. Suction ring placement + IOP elevation; 2. Microkeratome/femtosecond pass; 3. Flap reflection; 4. Stromal bed drying; 5. Ablation (eye tracker on); 6. Irrigation of interface; 7. Flap replacement; 8. Smoothing + drying; 9. Verify seating (no striae); 10. Patient instructed not to rub
Advantages vs PRK
Faster visual recovery (1-2 days); minimal pain; less regression; easier retreatment (lift old flap)
Disadvantages vs PRK
Flap complications; more severe dry eye (corneal nerve cut); flap displacement risk (trauma); cannot use in very thin corneas; flap never fully adheres permanently
Ablation Profile Types
Standard (conventional): treat manifest refraction; does not account for HOAs; slightly increases SA (oblates cornea)
Wavefront-optimised (WFO): standard treatment + peripheral compensation to maintain prolate shape; reduces SA induction; most common current approach; does not treat pre-existing HOAs

Wavefront-guided (WFG): custom treatment based on patient-specific wavefront map; corrects pre-existing HOAs; best for eyes with significant HOAs; requires high-quality wavefront measurement; Contoura Vision adds topography

Topography-guided (TG): uses corneal topography to plan ablation; centres on corneal apex (visual axis); reduces irregular astigmatism and HOAs; FDA-approved Contoura Vision (OASIS trial: superior to wavefront-guided for some outcomes)
Retreatment
Indications: residual myopia/astigmatism/hyperopia causing symptoms; stable refraction for ≥3 months after primary procedure
LASIK retreat: lift old flap (even years later); preferred over cutting new flap (avoid double flap); surface ablation alternative if flap not liftable
PRK retreat: re-treat Bowman; must account for residual stromal depth; MMC mandatory; higher haze risk than primary
Enhancement rate: ~5-10% for standard corrections; higher for high corrections, hyperopia, thin corneas; must confirm sufficient residual stromal bed before proceeding
Photoablation: Complications
General Complications (Both PRK & LASIK)
Undercorrection: most common; from regression, nomogram error, poor wound healing; manage with re-treatment after stable ≥3 months
Overcorrection: less common; myopes → hyperopia (usually temporary; manage with glasses until stable); hyperopes → myopia

Dry eye: very common (LASIK >>  PRK > SMILE); corneal nerve transection → reduced sensation → reduced blink reflex + reduced tear production; treat aggressively with lubricants, cyclosporine A, punctal plugs; usually resolves 3-6 months; more persistent in LASIK
Optical aberrations: glare, halos, starbursts; from large pupil overlapping small OZ, increased SA (oblate cornea), decentration; wavefront-optimised ablations reduce but don't eliminate
Decentred ablation: pupil misidentification; large angle kappa; cornea irregular topography; asymmetric HOA; treatment: topography-guided retreatment; most challenging complication to manage
Central islands: area of insufficient ablation in centre; from beam inhomogeneity, debris; smooth topography; management: topography-guided retreatment; less common with modern lasers
Surface Ablation-Specific Complications

Corneal haze: subepithelial fibrosis; worse with higher corrections (>-6D), UV exposure, deeper ablation; prevent with MMC; treat with topical steroids + MMC retreatment; phototherapeutic keratectomy (PTK)

Persistent epithelial defect (PED): healing >5 days; recurrent corneal erosion; neurotrophic cornea; treat with bandage CL, preservative-free lubricants, autologous serum; AVOID epithelial debridement again

Sterile infiltrates: immune reaction (staphylococcal hypersensitivity most common); peripheral white infiltrates; no organisms on culture; treat with topical steroids; culture to exclude infectious keratitis first
LASIK-Specific Complications
Flap Complications
Incomplete flap: microkeratome stops mid-cut; abort procedure; use remaining flap; try again in 3 months (scar forms)
Thin/irregular flap: irregular cutting; abort; surface ablation later
Buttonhole: flap perforation over steep cornea (KC suspect?); abort; high irregular astigmatism; surface ablation 3-6 months later
Free cap: no hinge created; treat entire stroma; replace with correct orientation; mark 12 o'clock pre-op
Opaque bubble layer (OBL): from femtosecond gas bubbles; delays flap lift; usually dissipates in minutes; wait before treating; can cause eye tracker interference
Flap Striae
Wrinkles in flap; micro-striae (fine; no VA loss) vs macro-striae (significant; VA reduced); from improper replacement or flap displacement
Management: early (within 24h) = lift and reposition (irrigation + smoothing); late = lift + epithelial debridement of underside + reposition
Flap Dislocation
Trauma (even minor rubbing) can displace LASIK flap months-years post-op; flap never fully adheres
Management: surgical flap replacement under sterile conditions; irrigate debris; smooth; suture if necessary
Interface Complications
DLK (diffuse lamellar keratitis = Sands of Sahara): sterile interface inflammation; white granular cells between flap and stroma; Stage 1 (peripheral, <25%) → 4 (central with stromal melting)
DLK cause: bacterial endotoxin (instrument cleaning), meibomian secretions, blood, metallic particles, ICG
DLK treatment: Stage 1-2: intensive topical steroids q1-2h; Stage 3-4: lift flap, irrigate, steroids
Epithelial ingrowth: epithelial cells grow under flap; usually peripheral, innocuous; if central (dense) → reduce VA + cause irregular astigmatism → lift and remove; more common with retreatment

PISK (pressure-induced stromal keratitis): masquerades as DLK but from steroid-induced IOP rise (falsely low IOP measurement post-LASIK); treat by stopping steroids + IOP-lowering drops; IOP monitoring essential
Ectasia
Progressive corneal thinning and ectatic protrusion after LASIK/PRK; similar to keratoconus; from biomechanical instability

Risk factors: pre-existing ectasia/KC (absolute contraindication), thin cornea, RSB <250-300μm, high myopia, steep cornea, young age, forme fruste KC (ABCD tomography, BAD-D, Corvis TBI), abnormal biomechanics
Clinical: progressive myopia + astigmatism + VA loss + inferior steepening on topography; onset months to years post-LASIK
Management: spectacles/RGP lenses → scleral lenses → CXL (halts progression; FDA-approved for post-LASIK ectasia) → ICRS (Intacs, flatten cone) → DALK/PKP if severe
Femtosecond Lenticule Extraction (SMILE)
Principles & Technique

SMILE (small incision lenticule extraction): femtosecond laser (VisuMax, Zeiss) creates two intrastromal planes + small cap incision; lenticule of tissue between planes is manually extracted through 2-3mm incision; no flap, no excimer laser
Relex SMILE: approved for myopia up to -10D and astigmatism up to -5D; older RELEX-FLEx was full-flap version (largely abandoned)

Surgical steps: 1. Docking (suction cup attaches); 2. Femtosecond cuts (posterior lenticule surface, anterior cap surface, edge incision); 3. Tissue planes separated with special dissectors (double-hoe dissector); 4. Lenticule grasped and extracted through 2-3mm incision; 5. Pocket irrigated
Advantages vs LASIK
No flap: no flap displacement/dislocation risk; safer for contact sports, military, police
Less dry eye: corneal nerve preservation better (only anterior stromal nerves cut at small incision; LASIK cuts nerve ring around entire flap)
No flap striae, no OBL (usually), no epithelial ingrowth
Less HOA induction: preserves more anterior stromal biomechanics
Disadvantages vs LASIK
Longer learning curve; technically more difficult (extracting lenticule through small incision)
Limited retreatment options: no simple flap lift; retreatment = surface ablation (PRK on cap) or convert to 'SMILE flex' (open cap to LASIK-like flap)
No hyperopia correction (currently); limited to myopia and myopic astigmatism
Cannot combine with custom wavefront treatment (at present); no topography-guided SMILE available
Higher tissue bridge required: cap ≥100μm; cap + lenticule + residual bed ≥250μm
Outcomes & Complications
Outcomes comparable to LASIK for myopia/astigmatism in current literature; slightly slower early visual recovery; ultimately similar VA outcomes
Suction loss: most serious; if during cap cut → complete loss of lenticule orientation; attempt to re-dock vs abort
Opaque bubble layer: less common than LASIK femtosecond (smooth cap surface, no interface to create OBL)
Incomplete lenticule dissection: difficult extraction, torn lenticule → residual tissue → irregular astigmatism; avoid aggressive extraction
Cap perforation: rare; from dissector slip through thin tissue; manage conservatively; risk of haze/scarring
Refractive Surgery in Ocular & Systemic Disease
Ocular Conditions
Dry Eye Disease
Must treat and optimise before refractive surgery; aqueous tear deficiency + MGD require separate treatment
LASIK vs PRK in dry eye: LASIK worsens dry eye more severely; PRK preferred in moderate dry eye
SMILE: least dry eye among corneal refractive procedures; preferred if significant pre-existing dry eye
Management: cyclosporine A 0.05-0.1% for 3+ months pre-op; warm compresses/lid hygiene; omega-3; punctal plugs if severe; delay surgery until stable
Herpes Simplex / Zoster
Active HSV: absolute contraindication to any laser refractive surgery

Inactive HSV stromal keratitis: relative contraindication; risk of reactivation from UV/laser; if proceeding: confirm no active disease, acyclovir 400mg BD prophylaxis starting 2 weeks pre-op and continuing 3+ months; PRK preferred (no suction from LASIK)
VZV (HZO): similar precautions; higher reactivation risk; caution with any corneal procedure
Keratoconus
ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION to corneal laser refractive surgery (LASIK, PRK, SMILE) — all are contraindicated; risk of progressive ectasia

Exception: CXL-combined surface ablation (combined topography-guided PRK + same-day CXL = Athens Protocol); experimental; halts and partially reverses ectasia; NOT standard care; only for progressive KC with sufficient pachymetry
Mild forme fruste KC: also contraindicated; identify with tomography (BAD-D, TBI) before proceeding
Corneal Dystrophies
EBMD/map-dot-fingerprint: can cause irregular epithelium under LASIK flap; PRK preferred (removes abnormal epithelium); recurrent erosions post-op risk
Fuchs endothelial dystrophy: risk of corneal decompensation from refractive surgery; low endothelial cell count contraindication; consider RLE instead
Lattice dystrophy (TGFBI): PTK effective for anterior stromal lesions; PRK can be done if stroma adequate; LASIK risks flap irregularity
Post-PKP
High astigmatism common after keratoplasty; sequential treatment: suture removal + CL → AK/LASIK/PRK after refraction stable (≥1 year post-suture removal)
PRK preferred (no suction pressure of LASIK; no risk of corneal wound dehiscence); surface ablation + MMC; wait until graft stable
LASIK: possible but suction pressure risks wound dehiscence; use femtosecond laser preferred over microkeratome
Glaucoma
Post-LASIK IOP underestimation: cornea thinner/flatter → Goldmann tonometry reads low (4-6mmHg below true); critically important for glaucoma monitoring
Correction formulas: no perfect formula; dynamic contour tonometry (Pascal DCT) least affected by corneal properties; ORA (corneal-compensated IOP = IOPcc) more accurate post-refractive surgery
High IOP at LASIK suction ring: brief IOP >40mmHg during suction; safe for most; risk in pre-existing advanced glaucoma (optic nerve at risk); alternative: PRK or SMILE (lower peak IOP)
Stable glaucoma well-controlled: refractive surgery generally acceptable; unstable/advanced glaucoma = relative contraindication; document pre-op optic nerve status
Retinal Disease & Myopia
High myopia + lattice degeneration: treat symptomatic breaks before refractive surgery; asymptomatic lattice with holes: controversial (prophylactic laser often recommended pre-LASIK for high myopes)
Prior RD repair: scleral buckle can affect corneal topography (cylinder); wait until stable; PRK > LASIK (suction complications with buckle)
Refractive surgery does NOT increase RD risk beyond that already present from myopia itself; but patient should understand pre-existing RD risk from myopia
Amblyopia & Strabismus
Anisometropic amblyopia in children: refractive surgery can reduce anisometropia → improve amblyopia treatment (spectacle compliance poor); evidence emerging for paediatric PRK
Adults with amblyopia: will not improve BCVA with refractive surgery; counsel that surgery corrects refractive error but not amblyopia; UCVA improves but BCVA ceiling unchanged
Strabismus: monovision LASIK can destabilise accommodative esotropia (reduces accommodation convergence); screen for strabismus pre-op; binocular assessment essential
Systemic Conditions
HIV: well-controlled, normal immune function: acceptable; no major increased risk; caution if immunocompromised (CD4 <200 cells/μL = relative contraindication; poor wound healing, infection risk)

Diabetes mellitus: controlled DM with good HbA1c: acceptable; PRK preferred (slower healing with LASIK flap); counsel about slower visual recovery; dry eye more common; avoid if significant corneal neuropathy (DES questionnaire, corneal aesthesiometry)

Connective tissue/autoimmune: RA, SLE, Sjögren, vasculitis: generally contraindicated; risk of non-healing, peripheral ulcerative keratitis, severe dry eye, abnormal scarring; corneal melt reported post-LASIK; RLE may be safer alternative
Isotretinoin (Accutane): causes severe dry eye; discontinue ≥6 months before surgery; monitor dry eye aggressively post-op
Amiodarone: corneal deposits (verticillata) but do not interfere with ablation; not a contraindication; document deposits pre-op
Considerations After Refractive Surgery
IOL Calculations Post-Refractive Surgery
Triple Problem (post-myopic LASIK)

Problem 1 — Instrument measurement error: keratometer/Placido topography measure anterior surface, assume standard anterior/posterior ratio (0.82); post-LASIK anterior flattened but posterior unchanged → instrument underestimates total power
Problem 2 — Index of refraction error: standard refractive index (1.3375) assumes normal anterior/posterior ratio; altered by laser → calculated power wrong
Problem 3 — ELP (effective lens position) error: modern formulas predict ELP from K readings; flat post-LASIK K → predicted ELP too anterior → IOL power underestimated → hyperopic surprise
Solutions
ASCRS Online Post-Refractive IOL Power Calculator (www.ascrs.org/corneal-power): multiple methods averaged; best current approach; free to use
Barrett True-K formula: uses Gaussian optics; no pre-op data needed; among best performers; corrects for anterior/posterior corneal discrepancy
Shammas no-history method: requires only current post-operative K; useable when no pre-op data available
Double-K method: use post-op K for power calculation; use pre-op K for ELP prediction; corrects ELP error
Contact lens over-refraction (CL method): uses flat BCVA + CL parameters to back-calculate true corneal power; accurate but requires specific testing
Post-hyperopic LASIK: opposite errors; risk of myopic surprise; similar approach with specific methods
Clinical pearl: always aim slightly myopic (-0.5 to -0.75D) in post-LASIK eyes for cataract surgery; hyperopic surprise more bothersome; anti-LASIK patients fear glasses more for near
Glaucoma After Refractive Surgery
GAT underestimates IOP post-LASIK by 4-8mmHg; post-PRK: 2-3mmHg underestimate; post-RK: varies
Dynamic contour tonometry (Pascal): measures corneal-independent IOP; most accurate post-refractive surgery
ORA (IOPcc): compensated IOP from corneal-compensated algorithm; less affected than GAT
Inform all patients: IOP readings will be falsely low after surgery; must inform all future physicians; wear medical alert bracelet? important for emergency eye care
IOP correction formulas: Dougherty formula: adjusted IOP = GAT + 0.43 × ablation depth (μm) / 10; others available; imprecise; use dynamic tonometry when possible
Retinal Detachment After Refractive Surgery
RD risk in myopia is from myopia itself (axial length), NOT from laser refractive surgery per se; 1 Snellen line of myopia reduction does not change axial length or peripheral retinal pathology

Precautions: dilated fundus exam + scleral depression pre-op; treat significant peripheral lesions pre-op; advise any new floaters/flashes/curtain requires urgent assessment despite normal surgical outcome
RD repair post-LASIK: standard vitreoretinal approaches; scleral buckle: minor concern of suction pressure on flap area (usually not a problem)
Corneal Transplantation After Refractive Surgery
Post-LASIK ectasia: DALK preferred (preserves endothelium, avoids endothelial rejection); PKP if significant scarring or failed DALK
Post-LASIK PKP: flap increases surgical complexity; flap may dehisce at edge; scleral-tunnel incision preferred; predict HOA induction from flap edges
IOL calculations after post-refractive surgery keratoplasty: doubly difficult; use mean keratometry + conservative approaches; aim myopic
Intraocular Refractive Surgery
Phakic IOL (pIOL)
Background & Advantages
Implant added power lens inside the eye (between cornea and crystalline lens); does NOT remove corneal tissue; reversible (explantable)
Advantages: corrects higher myopia than LASIK (up to -20D); preserves corneal thickness + shape; better optical quality (no corneal aberrations induced); reversible; preserves accommodation
Types
Angle-supported AC IOL: historical (Baikoff, NuVita); angle fixation; endothelial cell loss, PAS, pupil ovalization; largely abandoned

Iris-fixated (iris-claw) AC IOL: Artisan/Verisyse (PMMA), Artiflex/Veriflex (foldable); enclavated to iris midperiphery; requires PI (to prevent pupillary block); good long-term safety data; risk of chronic endothelial cell loss; requires adequate ACD >3.2mm

Posterior chamber pIOL (Implantable Collamer Lens, ICL/EVO ICL): most popular current pIOL; sulcus/sulcus fixation; KS-AquaPORT (central hole in lens) = no peripheral iridotomy needed; UV-absorbing collamer material; myopia range -3 to -20D, astigmatism up to -6D
ICL Selection & Sizing

Vault: distance between ICL optic and anterior crystalline lens; ideal 250-750μm on Scheimpflug; too low vault (<100μm) → cataract formation from crystalline lens contact; too high vault (>1000μm) → angle closure from iris bombe
ICL sizing: white-to-white (WTW) + sulcus-to-sulcus (STS) UBM measurement; ICL size = STS or WTW + 0.5mm; if UBM available, use STS-based sizing
Toric ICL: for myopic astigmatism; must align with steep axis; mark with pendular marker before surgery; rotational stability excellent (99% within 5° at 1 year)
Patient Selection
Age: 21-45 years (stable refraction, pre-presbyopia); older → consider RLE
Myopia: -3 to -20D (ideal range for ICL: -6 to -15D; best outcomes)
ACD: ≥2.8mm (minimum, prefer ≥3.0mm) for ICL; ≥3.2mm for iris-claw
Endothelial cell count: ≥2000 cells/mm² (ideally ≥2500); insufficient endothelium → risk of bullous keratopathy
No pre-existing cataract, narrow angles, uveitis; clear media required
Contraindications: KC (absolute), shallow AC, poor endothelial count, cataract, uncontrolled glaucoma
Complications
Cataract: anterior subcapsular; from chronic ICL contact with crystalline lens (inadequate vault); more common in early non-KS-PORT lens; manage by explanting ICL early
Pupillary block glaucoma (iris-claw, old designs): PI essential pre-op; EVO ICL KS-AquaPORT eliminates need for PI
Angle closure: excessive vault; ICL too large; pupil block; treat with iridoplasty or explant ICL + replace smaller size
Endothelial cell loss: ~1-2% per year (more with angle-supported AC IOLs; less with posterior chamber ICL); monitor annually with specular microscopy
Under/overcorrection: phakic IOL power can be exchanged; more flexible than LASIK
Refractive Lens Exchange (RLE / Clear Lens Extraction)
Indications
High hyperopia (>+4D) where corneal surgery less predictable and safe
High myopia (>-10 to -12D) beyond ICL or corneal laser range
Presbyopia with high refractive error (eliminates cataracts + corrects presbyopia simultaneously)
Patient >50 years with myopia who wants spectacle independence (no accommodation remaining; trades clear intermediate for improved near and distance with premium IOL)
Advantages
Eliminates future cataracts; opportunity for multifocal/EDOF IOL; permanent correction; IOL power exchangeable
Disadvantages
Loss of accommodation (irreversible in patients <45 years with any residual accommodation)
RD risk: higher in high myopia (thin retina, vitreous degeneration); most important concern; dilated fundus exam + prophylactic peripheral laser essential pre-op in myopes
PCO: Nd:YAG needed in 20-30% within 5 years
Intraocular procedure: endophthalmitis risk (0.05-0.1%)
IOL Selection for RLE
Hyperopic RLE: monofocal (best VA but reading glasses); multifocal/trifocal (spectacle independence; higher photic phenomena); EDOF (intermediate + partial near)
Myopic RLE: difficult target (high myopia = long AL = formula errors); use Wang-Koch AL adjustment; aim -0.5D; be conservative with premium IOLs (realistic expectations)
Consider patient age, occupation, lifestyle before IOL selection; bilateral RLE planning (simultaneous vs sequential)
Bioptics
Combination of pIOL + corneal laser ablation; pIOL corrects bulk of myopia, laser corrects residual refractive error + fine-tunes astigmatism
Advantage: each procedure within safe range; better predictability for extreme myopia; excellent optical quality
pIOL first → wait 3 months for refraction stability → PRK/LASIK for residual error; laser must account for post-pIOL corneal flattening
Treatment of Presbyopia
Theories of Accommodation

Helmholtz (classic): ciliary muscle contraction → zonule release → lens rounds (increases curvature) → power increase; ciliary muscle function preserved until late; lens stiffening = primary cause of presbyopia
Schachar (alternative): tension on equatorial zonules steepens lens centre while relaxing periphery; controversial; basis for scleral expansion procedures
Presbyopia cause: lens nucleus hardens (α-crystallin loss, protein denaturation, oxidation) → cannot change shape; NOT ciliary muscle failure
Accommodative Approaches

Scleral surgery (Scleral Expansion Bands, LaserACE): create scleral expansion to restore equatorial space for zonular function (Schachar theory); limited evidence; not widely adopted; small randomised trials show modest near improvement

Accommodating IOLs: Crystalens (single-optic vaulting mechanism; moves forward with accommodation); FluidVision (fluid fills optic on accommodation); limited true accommodation achieved; largely replaced by EDOF for presbyopia correction
Lens refilling: remove lens content, inject gel polymer to recreate elastic lens; early trials; challenges with sealing capsule opening
Non-Accommodative Approaches
Monovision
Dominant eye corrected for distance; non-dominant eye for near (-1.25 to -1.75D); brain suppresses blur from each eye based on task
Trial with CLs essential before surgical monovision; 30-40% patients cannot tolerate; reduced stereoacuity (important for night driving)
Mini-monovision: smaller anisometropia (-0.75 to -1.25D); better stereo; less near benefit; compromise
Can be combined with LASIK, PRK, RLE, ICL; straightforward to plan and execute
Multifocal Corneal Ablations (PresbyLASIK)
Central add (SUPRACOR): steepen central 2-3mm for near (myopic add); peripheral distance correction; reduces contrast sensitivity
Peripheral add: steepen peripheral annular zone; theoretically less visual quality compromise
Both: outcome variable; younger presbyopes better; patients must accept reduced contrast sensitivity and some halos; not widely adopted in US
Conductive Keratoplasty (CK)
Radiofrequency energy applied to peripheral cornea (ring pattern) → collagen shrinkage → central steepening → myopic correction for near
Used for distance-corrected hyperopic presbyopes; creates monovision in non-dominant eye
Regression over 1-2 years; repeat treatment needed; largely historical now
Pharmacological Presbyopia Treatment

Pilocarpine 1.25% (Vuity, AbbVie): creates pupil miosis → increased depth of focus; FDA-approved 2021; once daily; 6-hour effect; dim light/night vision trade-off; not for angle-closure risk; side effects: brow ache, myopia shift, dim vision
Carbachol/brimonidine combinations in development
Corneal Inlays

Kamra inlay (AcuFocus): small-aperture (1.6mm opening, 3.8mm disc); implanted in non-dominant eye stroma intrastromally (LASIK pocket or femtosecond pocket); increases depth of focus through pinhole effect; FDA-approved; currently not available commercially (withdrawn from market)
Raindrop inlay (ReVision Optics): reshapes corneal curvature; created exudate in corneal stroma → removed from market (corneal haze)
Presbia Flexivue: refractive annular inlay; near zone peripheral; not available in US
Multifocal & EDOF IOLs (See Ch 9/11 for full details)
Gold standard for bilateral spectacle independence; bifocal (PanOptix, ReSTOR), trifocal, EDOF (Symfony, Vivity), EDOF+toric (Symfony Toric)
Halos/glare trade-off; patient selection critical; neuroadaptation 3-6 months; IOL exchange if intolerant (rare)
Top 30 Exam Facts
Excimer laser: ArF 193nm; photoablation (cold); breaks molecular bonds; 0.25μm per pulse; Munnerlyn: depth = D × S² / 3
Femtosecond laser: 1053nm; photodisruption (ionisation + plasma); intrastromal cuts; used for LASIK flap + SMILE lenticule + AK
Normal cornea: prolate (Q ~-0.26); post-myopic LASIK: oblate → increases spherical aberration (Z₄⁰)
Wavefront HOAs: Z₃±¹ = coma (decentration); Z₄⁰ = spherical aberration (increases after LASIK); RMS >0.5μm = symptomatic
Corneal topography maps: axial = general overview; tangential = most sensitive for KC; elevation = best for ectasia screening (posterior elevation most important)
BAD-D >1.6 and TBI >0.29 = high ectasia risk; ABSOLUTE contraindications to LASIK
Minimum RSB (residual stromal bed) for LASIK: 250-300μm; minimum pre-op pachymetry for LASIK: ≥480-500μm total
Keratoconus: absolute contraindication to ALL corneal laser refractive surgery (LASIK, PRK, SMILE)
CXL Dresden protocol: epithelium off, 0.1% riboflavin + dextran, 3mW/cm² UV-A ×30 min; accelerated (9-45mW); halts KC progression; FDA-approved for post-LASIK ectasia
PRK vs LASIK: PRK = no flap, better biomechanics, more dry eye long-term; LASIK = faster recovery, more dry eye early, flap risks; SMILE = least dry eye, no flap, no hyperopia correction
IFIS-like reaction in SMILE: miosis from prostaglandin release during femtosecond docking — less problematic than LASIK's IFIS from systemic alpha-blockers
DLK (diffuse lamellar keratitis): sterile interface inflammation; Stages 1-4; Stage 1-2 → intensive topical steroids; Stage 3-4 → lift flap + irrigate
PISK: mimics DLK but caused by steroid-induced IOP rise; check IOP; stop steroids + IOP-lowering drops; distinguish by IOP measurement under flap
Epithelial ingrowth: beneath LASIK flap; treat if central/dense (VA impact) by lifting and removing; more common after retreatment
Haze prevention: MMC 0.02% for >-5D surface ablation, retreatment PRK; applied 12-60 seconds post-ablation; rinse thoroughly
SMILE retreatment: PRK on cap preferred; or convert to SMILE-flex (open cap to LASIK-like); cannot lift lenticule pocket as flap for standard re-ablation
Post-LASIK IOP underestimation: 4-6mmHg below true IOP; Pascal DCT or IOPcc less affected; warn all patients and future physicians
Post-LASIK IOL calculation triple error: instrument error + index error + ELP error → hyperopic surprise; use ASCRS calculator or Barrett True-K; aim slightly myopic (-0.5D)
ICL vault: ideal 250-750μm; <100μm → anterior subcapsular cataract; >1000μm → angle closure; measure on Pentacam post-op
EVO ICL (KS-AquaPORT): central hole eliminates need for laser peripheral iridotomy; reduces pupillary block risk vs older ICL designs
ICL sizing: WTW + 0.5mm (or STS-based); undersized → low vault → cataract; oversized → high vault → angle closure
Phakic IOL contraindications: keratoconus, ACD <2.8mm, endothelial count <2000, cataract, narrow angles, uncontrolled glaucoma
RLE high myopia risk: RD risk is highest concern; dilated fundus + peripheral laser treatment pre-op essential; loss of accommodation (irreversible)
Monovision: trial with CLs before surgical commitment; dominant eye = distance; non-dominant = near (-1.25 to -1.75D); 30-40% cannot tolerate; reduces stereopsis
Presbyopia drugs: pilocarpine 1.25% (Vuity); FDA-approved 2021; miosis → depth of focus; 6h duration; dim light trade-off; not if narrow angles
Post-LASIK ectasia management: CXL (halts progression); ICRS (Intacs improve shape); scleral lenses (optical rehabilitation); DALK/PKP if severe

Radial keratotomy late effects: progressive hyperopia (myopic drift then hyperopic), diurnal fluctuation, weakened cornea; IOL calculation extremely difficult post-RK; flatter K + no pre-op data = worst calculation scenario
AK/LRI coupling: incision flattens one meridian + steepens perpendicular; coupling ratio ~1:1 at limbus; less precise than toric IOL for >1.5D astigmatism

HSV and refractive surgery: active HSV = absolute contraindication; inactive stromal keratitis = relative contraindication; acyclovir prophylaxis 2 weeks pre-op and 3 months post-op if proceeding; PRK preferred over LASIK
Toric IOL rotation: 1° rotation = 3.3% correction loss; 30° rotation = total loss of effect; mark steep meridian at slit lamp pre-op with patient sitting upright
